verses.commentary: Jesus leaves the temple — final departure. Disciples point to the buildings — the magnificent Herodian temple (Josephus describes it in luminous detail). Tourists impressed by what Jesus has just declared desolate.
'Not one stone will be left on another that will not be thrown down.' Total destruction predicted. Fulfilled in AD 70 by Titus' legions. The cultic center will end.

Mount of Olives — facing temple from the east. Disciples ask privately: 'When will these things be? What is the sign of your coming (parousia, παρουσία) and of the end of the age (synteleia tou aionos, συντέλεια τοῦ αἰῶνος)?' Three questions blended; Jesus' answer interleaves the AD 70 destruction and the final coming.
'Watch out that no one deceives you.' Opening with deception-warning. Apocalyptic discourse is itself fertile ground for false interpretation.
Many will come in my name claiming 'I am the Christ' and deceive many. False messiahs proliferate. Anticipation breeds counterfeits.
Wars and rumors of wars — do not be alarmed. These must occur. End not yet. Disturbances are precursors, not the climax.
Nation against nation, kingdom against kingdom. Famines and earthquakes in various places. The standard apocalyptic checklist — yet 'these are merely the beginning of birth pains.'
Birth pains (odinon, ὠδίνων) — first stage of a long labor. The new age comes in agony. Jewish apocalyptic imagery.
Disciples handed over to tribulation, killed, hated by all nations because of his name. Christian suffering is structural, not incidental. 'Because of me' — Christology is the persecuting trigger.
Many will fall away (skandalisthesontai, σκανδαλισθήσονται), betray each other, hate each other. Internal community fracture under pressure. The greatest danger is from within.
False prophets multiply and lead many astray. The crisis of true and false within the church will be acute.
Lawlessness (anomia, ἀνομία) multiplies, love (agape, ἀγάπη) of many grows cold (psygesetai, ψυγήσεται). The temperature of love drops in the climate of evil. Love is environmental.
'But the one who endures (hypomeinas, ὑπομείνας) to the end will be saved.' Endurance as evidence of genuine faith. Same logic as 10:22.

'This gospel of the kingdom will be proclaimed throughout the whole world (oikoumene, οἰκουμένῃ) as a testimony to all nations — and then the end will come.' Global proclamation precedes the consummation. Missiology is eschatology.

'Abomination of desolation' (bdelygma tes eremoseos, βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως, Daniel 9:27, 11:31, 12:11) standing in the holy place. Originally Antiochus IV's altar to Zeus in 168 BC; Jesus reapplies. Possibly the Roman standards in AD 70, or eschatological. 'Let the reader understand' — narratorial nudge.
Those in Judea — flee to the mountains. The historical instruction for AD 70 (Eusebius reports Christians fled to Pella before the siege).
On the rooftop — do not go down to take things. Flat-roofed Mediterranean homes had outside stairs. Flee directly.
In the field — do not turn back for your cloak. Speed essential; possessions must be abandoned.
Woe to pregnant and nursing women — flight is harder for them. Compassion expressed amid urgency.
Pray flight not be in winter (impassable terrain) or on a Sabbath (limited travel). Practical jewish-context concerns.
Great tribulation (thlipsis megale, θλῖψις μεγάλη) unlike any from creation's beginning, nor ever will be. Hyperbolic apocalyptic language — historically tied to AD 70 destruction.
If those days had not been cut short, no flesh would survive. For the elect's sake, they are shortened. Divine mercy truncates the suffering.
Then if anyone says 'Here is the Christ' or 'There' — do not believe. Localized messianic claims during chaos are deceptions.
False christs and false prophets — great signs and wonders — to deceive even (if possible) the elect. The deception arsenal includes miracles. Signs alone do not validate.
'I have told you beforehand.' Prepared disciples are forewarned disciples.
If they say 'In the wilderness' — do not go out. 'In the inner rooms' — do not believe. The true coming is not local or hidden.
Like lightning that flashes from east to west — so the coming of the Son of Man. Universal visibility, instantaneous. No need to be told it has happened — everyone will see.
'Wherever the corpse is, there the eagles (or vultures) will gather.' Cryptic proverb — possibly judgment image (Roman eagles over Jerusalem corpse?). Indicates inevitability.

Immediately after that tribulation — sun darkened, moon will not give light, stars fall from heaven, powers of the heavens shaken. Cosmic imagery (Isaiah 13:10; 34:4; Ezekiel 32:7) — Old Testament regularly uses these for political-judgment events. Symbolic destabilization.
Sign of the Son of Man appears. Tribes of earth mourn. Son of Man coming on clouds of heaven (Daniel 7:13) — vindicated and enthroned.
Angels with great trumpet (compare Isaiah 27:13) gather the elect from the four winds (the entire earth). Eschatological harvest.
Fig tree parable: when its branch becomes tender, leaves come out — summer is near. Read the signs. The kingdom approach is readable.
When you see all these things, know that he is near, at the very doors. Imminence emphasized.

'This generation (genea, γενεά) will not pass away until all these things take place.' Contested — possibly the AD 70 generation (the temple-destruction prophecy), possibly the Jewish people, possibly the generation that sees the final signs. Each reading has interpretive weight.
Heaven and earth pass away; my words never pass away. Creation transient, his words permanent. Christology of eternal word.

Of that day and hour — no one knows, not even angels in heaven nor the Son, but the Father only. Date-setting forbidden by Jesus himself. The Son's earthly limit on this knowledge is theologically striking.
Days of Noah — paradigm. People absorbed in normal life, oblivious to impending judgment.
Eating, drinking, marrying, giving in marriage — ordinary activity. Sin is not specified; normality is the indictment.
Did not know until the flood swept them all away. Ignorance is not innocence. The Son of Man's coming will be the same — sudden against a backdrop of normalcy.
Two in a field — one taken, one left. The taking is judicial removal or rescue (Greek can read either way). Sudden separation amid identical-looking lives.
Two women grinding — same pattern. The dividing line runs through ordinary work, not through visible status.
'Watch therefore (gregoreite, γρηγορεῖτε) — you do not know what day your Lord is coming.' Watchfulness is the posture. Uncertainty about timing is the design.
If the householder had known the watch when the thief was coming — would have watched. Apologetic for ignorance does not work after the loss.
Be ready — Son of Man comes at an hour you do not expect. Readiness is constant; the unexpected element is structural.
Faithful and wise servant — put in charge to give food at proper time. The role of leadership defined: feed the household.
Blessed is the servant whom the master finds doing this when he comes. Discovery in service is the test.
He will set him over all his possessions. Faithful management at small scale earns expanded responsibility.
Wicked servant assumes 'my master delays' — begins different behavior. Delay as license for license.
Begins to beat fellow servants, eat and drink with drunkards. Abuse of subordinates and indulgence of appetite — leadership corruption pattern.
Master comes on a day not expected, at an hour not known. Surprise return.

Cuts him in two (dichotomeo, διχοτομέω — severe judgment image) and assigns his portion with the hypocrites. Weeping and gnashing of teeth — the refrain. Leadership has accountability commensurate with privilege.
